text: What is a rosé wine?
options: A wine made only from Pinot Noir
A wine with brief skin contact with red grapes
A blend of red and white wine
explanation: Most rosé wine is made by allowing brief skin contact with red grape skins, giving it a pink colour.

text: Which of the following is a sparkling wine?
explanation: Prosecco is an Italian sparkling wine made from the Glera grape using the tank method.

text: Sherry is an example of which type of wine?
explanation: Sherry is a fortified wine from the Jerez region of southern Spain.

text: A wine labelled 'sweet' will have:
explanation: Sweet wines contain noticeable levels of residual sugar, giving them a sweet taste.

text: What gives red wine its colour?
options: Contact with grape skins during fermentation
explanation: Red wine gets its colour from the anthocyanin pigments in the skins of red grape varieties during maceration.

text: Which is NOT a type of wine?
explanation: Distilled products are spirits, not wine. Wine can be still, sparkling, or fortified.

text: Which grape variety produces wines with aromas of blackcurrant?
explanation: Cabernet Sauvignon is strongly associated with blackcurrant (cassis) aromas.

text: Pinot Grigio is typically:
explanation: Pinot Grigio from northern Italy is typically a light, dry, crisp white wine with subtle fruit flavours.

text: Merlot wines are generally described as:
options: Very high in tannin with green flavours
Soft, round, with plum and red fruit
explanation: Merlot produces soft, approachable wines with plum, red cherry, and smooth tannins.

text: Riesling can produce wines that are:
options: Dry, off-dry, or sweet depending on the style
explanation: Riesling is a versatile grape that can make wines across the sweetness spectrum, from bone dry to very sweet.

text: Which grape variety is associated with light-bodied red wines with cherry and strawberry flavours?
explanation: Pinot Noir typically produces light to medium-bodied reds with red fruit flavours like cherry and strawberry.

text: Syrah/Shiraz is known for producing wines with:
explanation: Syrah/Shiraz produces medium to full-bodied reds with dark fruit, black pepper, and spice.

text: Chardonnay can produce wines that range from:
options: Light and crisp to full-bodied and oaky, depending on winemaking
explanation: Chardonnay is highly versatile. Unoaked versions are crisp and citrusy; oaked versions are rich and buttery.

text: Which grape variety is most commonly associated with Bordeaux red blends?
explanation: Cabernet Sauvignon is the dominant red grape of the Bordeaux Left Bank, used in the region's famous blends.

text: What does 'vintage' mean on a wine label?
options: The year the grapes were harvested
explanation: The vintage on a wine label indicates the year in which the grapes used to make the wine were harvested.

text: If a wine has high acidity, it will taste:
explanation: High acidity gives wine a fresh, crisp, mouth-watering quality.

text: What happens if grapes are harvested very late?
options: They accumulate more sugar, producing sweeter or higher alcohol wines
explanation: Late-harvested grapes have higher sugar levels, which can result in sweeter wines or wines with higher alcohol.

text: The alcohol content of most table wines is typically:
explanation: Most still table wines fall in the 8-15% ABV range, depending on grape ripeness and winemaking style.

text: What happens to wine if it is stored in a warm place?
options: It can age prematurely and develop off-flavours
explanation: Heat accelerates chemical reactions in wine, causing premature ageing, loss of freshness, and potential faults.

text: Why should wine bottles be stored on their side?
options: To keep the cork moist and prevent air entering
explanation: Storing bottles on their side keeps the cork in contact with the wine, preventing it from drying out and letting air in.

text: A wine that has turned brown and tastes of vinegar is likely:
explanation: Excessive browning and vinegar-like aromas indicate the wine has been damaged by oxidation and acetic acid bacteria.

text: At what temperature should rosé wine be served?
explanation: Rosé wines are best served chilled at around 7-10°C to highlight their freshness and fruit character.

text: What is the purpose of decanting a wine?
options: To separate the wine from sediment and allow it to breathe
explanation: Decanting separates wine from any sediment and exposes it to air, which can help open up aromas and flavours.

text: How long can an opened bottle of still wine typically last in the fridge?
explanation: Once opened, still wine begins to oxidise. It will typically remain drinkable for 1-3 days if refrigerated.

text: Which type of wine pairs best with grilled steak?
explanation: The bold flavours and tannins of a full-bodied red like Cabernet Sauvignon complement the richness of grilled steak.

text: A crisp, dry white wine pairs well with:
explanation: Crisp, dry whites like Sauvignon Blanc complement the delicate flavours of fresh seafood and salads.

text: What is a general principle of food and wine pairing?
options: Sweet wines only go with savoury food
White wine should never be served with meat
Always pair red wine with fish
Match the weight of the wine to the weight of the food
explanation: Matching the body and intensity of the wine with the richness of the food is a fundamental pairing principle.

text: Sweet wines pair well with:
explanation: Sweet wines can complement spicy dishes by cooling the palate, and they pair naturally with desserts.

text: Which wine would pair best with a creamy pasta dish?
explanation: An oaked Chardonnay has the body and richness to match creamy pasta sauces.

text: High-acid wines pair well with:
explanation: High acidity in wine acts as a palate cleanser, cutting through the richness of fatty and oily foods.
